entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
entities.label: General Corpus source XML for EGilds
notes: The file is 1,619,654 bytes at the recorded CME submodule commit.
It contains 8 DIV1, 87 DIV2, 97 DIV3, 1,532 P, 72 L, 658 PB, 916 ITEM, 1,127 NOTE1, and 871 FOREIGN elements.
The repository file itself has no independent claim to be a new transcription of any archival witness.

entities.description: The electronic text identified as EGilds, published by the University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative in 1999 and currently displayed in the Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse.
entities.label: University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative encoding of English Gilds
notes: The header says the volume was keyboarded by an entity rendered as 'Chrisian' and met an error threshold of one character in 20,000; that name has not been silently corrected or identified.

The current U-M item-information panel displays London, Oxford University Press, 1870, while the embedded source description says first published 1870, reprinted 1963, and supplies a 1963 copy-specific call number. The immediate-carrier relation follows the embedded source description and preserves the public display date as a discovery-layer mismatch rather than treating it as proof that U-M keyed the 1870 issue.
The header claims that all print material, including front matter, was included, but structural comparison with the 1870 scan shows that the glossarial index and index on pp. 463–483 are absent.
The XML also lacks the new-reference leaf promised by the retained August 1892 notice; FRONT contains only the title page, contents, Lucy Toulmin Smith's introduction, and Brentano's essay.

The language declaration omits Middle English even though Middle English is a principal documentary language, and its Old English, German, and Greek labels chiefly describe quotations or editorial discussion rather than the collection's principal language.

rights.basis: The current U-M item states that the materials are in the public domain and applies the DPLA No Copyright–United States statement.
rights.component: The U-M digital collection item as currently presented
notes: This records the current provider assertion; it is not a worldwide legal conclusion about every underlying archival document or reproduction.

rights.basis: The embedded availability statement permits personal, research, teaching, and linking uses with notice but forbids redistribution by commercial and nonprofit content providers.
rights.component: The legacy electronic text distribution terms embedded in the XML header
notes: The legacy terms conflict in practical effect with the current U-M No Copyright–United States presentation and are preserved as a separate historical rights layer rather than silently reconciled.

entities.description: A distinct EETS issue reprinted from the 1870 plates with a small set of added notes and corrections by Lucy Toulmin Smith.
entities.label: English Gilds, corrected plate reprint, 1892
notes: Lucy Toulmin Smith's dated notice says that the original Public Record Office bundles had been entirely rearranged, promises a new-reference leaf and longer corrections at the front, and identifies two additions on p. 348 and one on p. 431.
The Berkeley Law Library catalog independently describes the 1892 issue as reprinted from the 1870 plates with a few notes added.

bibliographic.citation: Joshua Toulmin Smith, ed., English Gilds: The Original Ordinances of More than One Hundred Early English Gilds, completed with an introduction and glossary by Lucy Toulmin Smith and with a preliminary essay by Lujo Brentano, Early English Text Society, Original Series 40 (London: N. Trübner, 1870), cxcix, 483 pp.
bibliographic.publisher: N. Trübner for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 40
bibliographic.title: English Gilds

entities.description: The first printed edition of the edited documentary compilation, including Lucy Toulmin Smith's introduction and glossary, Lujo Brentano's preliminary essay, the three documentary parts, two appendices, and the general index.
entities.label: English Gilds, first edition, 1870
notes: Joshua Toulmin Smith completed Parts I and II apart from minor press corrections and short notes; Part III was in type but required substantial collation, notes, and abstracts when he died in April 1869.
Lucy Toulmin Smith completed the editorial work, wrote the introduction, compiled the glossary with advice from Richard Morris, and supplied most remaining collation and annotation.
The 1870 copy inspected does not contain the August 1892 notice or the later additions preserved in the XML.

rights.basis: The edition was first published in 1870, and the current U-M presentation of the materials applies No Copyright–United States.
rights.component: The 1870 printed edition text and typography
notes: This does not determine rights in newly created scans, catalog descriptions, or repository-supplied archival images outside the United States.

entities.description: A modern archival grouping of many separate guild returns, writs, and related records, not a single codex or manuscript work. English Gilds prints all forty-nine English returns selected for Part I and translates or excerpts further material in Part II, but it is not a complete edition of the full current series.

historical_designations: Miscellaneous Rolls, Tower Records, bundle CCCVIII
Miscellaneous Rolls, Tower Records, bundle CCCIX
Miscellaneous Rolls, Tower Records, bundle CCCX
holding.notes: The current aggregate reference is supported by modern scholarship; an item-level concordance from Toulmin Smith's bundle and skin numbers to current references has not been recovered.

entities.label: 1388–89 Chancery guild returns, TNA C 47/38–46
notes: The 1892 notice says the former three bundles no longer existed as arranged and required new references.
This entity is explicitly an archival source group and must not be cited as if all printed documents shared one physical witness.

physicalDescription.extent: many separately sized skins, membranes, and papers
notes: Toulmin Smith described 549 skins or membranes across the three former bundles and emphasized their varied sizes and materials.
That historical count is not asserted as the current item count of C 47/38–46.
physicalDescription.support: chiefly parchment, with some paper reported

entities.description: The fourteenth-century civic roll used for the Winchester text in Part III. The 1870 edition says Winchester's mayor, council, and town clerk lent the original for collation.
entities.label: Winchester Olde Usages roll
notes: The exact current repository and shelfmark were not established from a current institutional catalog.
A collateral French version formerly at Winchester College is related evidence, not the source used for the printed English text.

physicalDescription.dimensions: approximately 5 ft 2.75 in long and 7.5–8 in wide
physicalDescription.extent: two skins sewn together and written on both sides
notes: The edition reports wear, damage, and passages pointing back to older usage or an older exemplar.

entities.description: The principal municipal volume used for Part III's Worcester ordinances, one of four original Worcester Corporation volumes lent to the editors.

holding.notes: The 1870 edition documents custody by Worcester Corporation; the current Worcester City Collection is held at The Hive, but the exact modern item reference has not been identified.

entities.label: Worcester ordinances volume formerly titled Ordinances, Edward IV

notes: The first three quires contain eighty-two ordinances dated 1467; later hands fill the remaining quires, and the printed edition selects rather than reproduces the whole volume.

entities.description: Bristol's official town chronicle begun by town clerk Robert Ricart in 1479 and continued by later civic officers. English Gilds prints only its fourth part, The Office of the Mayor of Bristol, after Lucy Toulmin Smith collated the proof against the original.
entities.label: The Maire of Bristowe Is Kalendar
notes: The 1870 edition numbers paragraphs for convenience and omits the manuscript's few side notes.
No complete public digital facsimile was found; a single institutional illustration is not treated as whole-witness access.

rights.basis: The exact catalog record and contact route are public, but no blanket open license for complete manuscript images was found.
rights.component: New photographs, scans, or supplied reproductions of Ricart's Kalendar

entities.description: A nineteenth-century working manuscript supplied to Joshua Toulmin Smith. It mediated Bristol material in Part II and initially mediated The Office of the Mayor of Bristol in Part III before Lucy Toulmin Smith obtained access to the original Ricart Kalendar.
entities.label: Charles Innes Pocock's manuscript volume of Bristol record extracts
notes: This was not a single-source transcript: the edition also used it for Fullers, Kalenders, and Ringers records in Part II.
Its present location has not been identified.

access.accessMethod: No exact current catalog record or complete public page-image source was identified
access.entityLabel: Tettenhall customary copy made from an older original in 1604

entities.description: The older copy that supplied the base text for the Tettenhall customary in Part III. Its heading says it was copied on 22 July 1604 from a copy taken from the original.
entities.label: Tettenhall customary copy made from an older original in 1604
notes: Joshua Toulmin Smith received this incomplete older copy through T. W. Cooper.
The wording establishes at least one earlier copying stage; it does not justify calling the 1604 copy the lost original customary.

access.accessMethod: No exact current catalog record or complete public page-image source was identified
access.entityLabel: Later Tettenhall customary copy supplied by Mr Neve

dateStatements.label: paper date evidence
dateStatements.notes: The edition reports an 1817 watermark; this dates the paper, not necessarily the textual exemplar.

entities.description: A fuller later paper copy used to fill and correct the older Tettenhall text in English Gilds.
entities.label: Later Tettenhall customary copy supplied by Mr Neve
notes: The copy reported that four separate copies had been made in 1604, including one subscribed by a jury and based on more ancient material.
Its present location has not been identified.

entities.label: Rawlinson manuscript formerly designated Misc. 1370

entities.description: The intermediary copy supplied for Appendix I and twice collated against the Bodleian manuscript by G. Parker.
entities.label: G. Parker's working copy of Rawlinson Misc. 1370

next_steps: Inspect University of Michigan call number 820.6 E1 no.40 1963 or obtain full images of its front matter and pp. 348 and 431.
Compare the 1963 copy against both the cached 1870 scan and a verified 1892 copy.
Record whether the missing reference leaf was absent from the 1963 carrier or omitted only during encoding.
openQuestions.question: Which exact 1963 physical issue did Michigan encode, and did that copy descend directly from the 1892 corrected plates and contain the promised reference-update leaf?

next_steps: Obtain current piece and item lists for C 47/38–46 from The National Archives.
Crosswalk every Part I and relevant Part II source note by place, incipit, language, material, and former number.
Preserve many-to-many mappings rather than forcing one printed unit to one modern item.
openQuestions.question: How do Toulmin Smith's obsolete bundle, skin, and membrane labels map item by item to current TNA references within C 47/38–46?

next_steps: Send repository enquiries quoting the edition's physical descriptions and historical custodians.
Ask Bodleian staff for a Rawlinson Miscellaneous old-to-current shelfmark crosswalk.
Ask Worcestershire Archive and Archaeology Service to identify the 54-leaf six-quire volume historically bound as Ordinances, Edward IV.
Trace Winchester and Tettenhall provenance without labeling unlocated witnesses lost.
openQuestions.question: What are the exact current shelfmarks, survival states, and consultation routes for the Winchester roll, Worcester ordinance volume, both Tettenhall copies, and Bodleian Rawlinson Misc. 1370?

next_steps: Extract every Part II source note and build an item-level source inventory by town and document.

Verify current institutional catalog records for the York, Beverley, Hull, Lancaster, Chesterfield, Lincoln, Stamford, Ludlow, Worcester, Stratford, Coventry, Birmingham, Cambridge, Bristol, Reading, Exeter, and Berwick material.
Model working copies and printed intermediaries separately wherever the editor did not use the original.
openQuestions.question: Which exact archival witnesses underlie each translated, excerpted, or English document in Part II beyond the 1388–89 Chancery returns?

next_steps: Request clarification from the University of Michigan Library about the status of legacy availability statements embedded in downloadable CME texts.
Do not treat the repository's general output license as automatically relicensing upstream transcription content.
Keep copyright, provider terms, and repository policy as separate assertions until clarified.
openQuestions.question: How should the XML's legacy redistribution prohibition be reconciled operationally with the current U-M No Copyright–United States statement and the repository's general CC BY-SA output policy?

reviewNotes: The 1870 compilation, the 1892 corrected plate reprint, the 1963 carrier, the U-M encoding, the repository XML, the scan, and the archival witnesses are modeled as distinct entities.
The 1963-to-1892 relation is supported at medium confidence by the retained 1892 note and additions; it is not upgraded to confirmed without inspecting the 1963 volume.
Part I and Part II source statements do not justify treating TNA C 47/38–46 as one physical manuscript or the printed selection as a complete archival census.
Historical shelfmarks and custodians are retained without inventing current catalog identifiers.
The cache contains only the complete public 1870 edition and its provider metadata/scan data; no fragmentary manuscript image was substituted for a complete witness facsimile.

lineage.summary: The checked-in EGilds XML is a General Corpus copy of the University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative encoding published in 1999 from an Early English Text Society reprint dated 1963. It represents Joshua Toulmin Smith and Lucy Toulmin Smith's edited documentary compilation English Gilds, Lujo Brentano's independent preliminary essay, and Richard Morris's grammatical appendix, while keeping these modern contributions distinct from the many medieval and early-modern records. The encoding retains Lucy Toulmin Smith's August 1892 plate-reprint notice and the three additions that notice identifies, so the 1963 carrier is probably downstream of the corrected 1892 issue; the exact 1963 exemplar has not been inspected. A complete public scan of an uncorrected 1870 copy is cached and proves that the XML omits the 1870 glossarial index and general index on pp. 463–483, as well as the reference-update leaf promised in the 1892 notice. The documentary sources are heterogeneous: Parts I and portions of Part II draw on many 1388–89 Chancery guild returns now grouped in TNA C 47/38–46, while Part III and Appendix I derive from separate Winchester, Worcester, Bristol, Tettenhall, and Bodleian witnesses, sometimes through working transcripts. Exact modern shelfmarks and complete public facsimiles remain unresolved for several of those sources.

catalogSummary.author: Named and anonymous medieval and early-modern documentary creators; compiled and edited by Joshua Toulmin Smith and Lucy Toulmin Smith
catalogSummary.date: Documentary sources chiefly fourteenth–fifteenth centuries, with later records and copies; compilation first published 1870 and corrected 1892
catalogSummary.editor: Joshua Toulmin Smith; completed by Lucy Toulmin Smith
catalogSummary.region: England, especially London, Norfolk, Winchester, Worcester, Bristol, and Tettenhall, with records from many additional towns
catalogSummary.title: English Gilds

catalogingSubject.label: English Gilds
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Joshua Toulmin Smith and Lucy Toulmin Smith's 1870 edited documentary compilation, including Lucy's introduction and glossary, Lujo Brentano's independent preliminary essay, three main documentary parts, two appendices, and an index, as transmitted through a corrected 1892 plate reprint and a 1963 EETS reprint to the U-M encoding. The historical documents are many distinct works and records with many creators and witnesses; the cataloging subject is not one homogeneous medieval work, one physical manuscript, the whole TNA C 47/38–46 series, the printed book as an artifact, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML. The XML represents the title page through Appendix II but omits the 1870 glossarial index and general index on pp. 463–483 and apparently omits the reference-update leaf promised in the retained August 1892 notice.

contentParts.label: Introduction by Lucy Toulmin Smith
contentParts.summary: Explains the edition's completion after Joshua Toulmin Smith's death, its selected documentary purpose, the editors' responsibilities, and the social character of English guilds.

contentParts.label: On the History and Development of Gilds, and the Origin of Trade-Unions
contentParts.summary: Lujo Brentano's independent five-part historical essay on guild origins, religious guilds, guild merchants, craft guilds, and trade unions.

contentParts.label: Part I: Returns, in English
notes: The forty-nine printed returns are editorial units, not necessarily forty-nine independent physical manuscripts under current archival arrangement.
contentParts.summary: Forty-nine numbered English-language guild returns from the January 1389 inquiry, chiefly concerning London, Norwich, Lynn, Wiggenhall, East Winch, and Oxborough, preceded by an editor's introduction.

contentParts.label: Part II: Illustrations of the Ordinances and Usages of Early English Gilds
notes: The nineteen DIV2 structures comprise one editor's introduction and eighteen top-level place/source groupings; many groupings contain multiple distinct documents, so no false item total is supplied.
contentParts.summary: A heterogeneous selection of writs, returns, translations, ordinances, charters, reports, oaths, inventories, and other guild records arranged under eighteen place or source groupings.

contentParts.label: Part III: Gilds and Municipal Bodies
notes: The four documentary items are counted in the child parts below; the parent item_count is zero to avoid double counting.
contentParts.summary: An editorial introduction followed by four separately sourced civic texts from Winchester, Worcester, Bristol, and Tettenhall.

contentParts.label: The Olde Usages of the City of Winchester
contentParts.summary: Middle English civic usages transcribed from a damaged fourteenth-century two-skin roll, with editorial note and grammatical commentary.

contentParts.label: The Ordinances of Worcester
contentParts.summary: Selected Worcester civic ordinances, principally the eighty-two ordinances of 1467, with a calendar, later material, and editorial note.

contentParts.label: The Office of the Mayor of Bristol
notes: The edition numbers paragraphs and omits a few manuscript side notes.

extent.description: The complete edition has nine high-level intellectual components: introduction, preliminary essay, three documentary parts, two appendices, glossarial index, and general index. The repository XML has eight DIV1 containers because it also encodes a contents division but omits the glossary and index; within those are 87 DIV2 and 97 DIV3 structures. Part I contains 49 numbered returns plus an editor's introduction, Part II contains 18 place/source groupings plus an editor's introduction, and Part III contains four records plus an editor's introduction. These edition and encoding structures are not a stable count of independent works or physical manuscripts. The 72 L elements are embedded or quoted lineation within an overwhelmingly prose compilation.

formStatements.description: A prose edited documentary collection with prose editorial matter, essay, notes, glossary, and index. The XML's 72 L elements represent embedded quotations or locally lineated material, not a verse-form collection.

A single comparative lexical quotation in editorial commentary
A single comparative or etymological quotation in the essay

notes: The cataloging subject is an edited selection and modern scholarly composite, not one medieval authored work.
The many historical records, printed editions, physical witnesses, digital encoding, repository artifact, and scan remain separate lineage layers.

The U-M header's language list is not propagated uncritically: Middle English is added from direct textual evidence, while Old English, German, and Greek are scoped as quotation rather than primary language.
The 72 XML L elements do not justify assigning verse form to this predominantly prose collection.
The glossarial index and general index are included in the complete edition boundary and content summary but explicitly marked absent from the repository XML.
No single current shelfmark is assigned to the Part II source set; its archival heterogeneity remains an item-level research problem.

summaries.value: English Gilds assembles selected returns, ordinances, customaries, charters, reports, and municipal records to document the organization and social practices of English religious, craft, and civic guilds. Part I prints forty-nine English returns from the 1388–89 royal inquiry, chiefly from London and Norfolk. Part II broadens the evidence through translated and English materials from towns across England. Part III presents four separate civic texts: Winchester's Olde Usages, Worcester ordinances, Robert Ricart's account of the Bristol mayor's office, and the Tettenhall customary. A fuller Norwich St George ordinance set and Richard Morris's grammatical notes form the two appendices. Lucy Toulmin Smith's introduction frames the documentary project, while Lujo Brentano's independent five-part essay argues a history from early guilds to trade unions. The complete 1870 edition also contains Lucy's glossarial index and a general index, both absent from the repository XML.

The 1870 volume comprises Lucy Toulmin Smith's introduction (pp. xi–xlviii); Lujo Brentano's preliminary essay (pp. xlix–cxcix); Part I, forty-nine English guild returns plus an editorial introduction (pp. 1–123); Part II, eighteen town or source groupings plus an editorial introduction (pp. 125–346); Part III, an editorial introduction and four civic records (pp. 347–442); Appendix I, Norwich St George ordinances (pp. 443–460); Appendix II, Richard Morris's grammatical notes (pp. 461–462); a glossarial index (pp. 463–478); and a general index (pp. 479–483). The XML encodes eight DIV1 units—contents, introduction, essay, Parts I–III, and Appendices I–II—but does not encode the last two printed components.

The edition's claim to contain ordinances of more than one hundred guilds is a title and marketing boundary, not a machine-countable number of independent works or witnesses. Part I has forty-nine numbered returns; Part II groups many documents under eighteen place headings; Part III has four separate source texts; Appendix I adds a fuller Norwich witness. Editorial introductions, translations, notes, parallel texts, glossarial entries, and index entries must not be counted as equivalent documentary works.

notes: The edition title continues with source, editor, introduction/glossary, and preliminary-essay responsibility statements; those are modeled as responsibilities rather than folded into the preferred title.
titles.value: English Gilds: The Original Ordinances of More than One Hundred Early English Gilds, Together with þe Olde Vsages of þe Cite of Wynchestre; the Ordinances of Worcester; the Office of the Mayor of Bristol; and the Costomary of the Manor of Tettenhall-Regis

work.author: Named and anonymous medieval and early-modern documentary creators; compiled and edited by Joshua Toulmin Smith and Lucy Toulmin Smith
work.dateDisplay: Documentary sources chiefly fourteenth–fifteenth centuries, with later records and copies; compilation first published 1870 and corrected 1892
work.editor: Joshua Toulmin Smith; completed by Lucy Toulmin Smith
